---
title: "Journal Entry 4: Chapters 1 and 2 Reflections"
date: "2026-03-27"
categories: [reflection, writing, introduction, related-work]
description: "Reflections on writing the Introduction and Related Work chapters"
---

![Writing reflection](../thumbnail.jpg)

## Reflections on Writing Chapters 1 and 2

In this journal entry, reflect on your experience writing the first two chapters
of your junior seminar research report.

### Chapter 1: Introduction

#### Writing Process

The introduction went through a significant revision cycle. My first draft jumped straight into technical content — Weibull distributions, Kijima virtual age, constraint programming — without giving the reader any on-ramp. Professor Kapfhammer's feedback pointed this out clearly: I needed plain-language introductions before the dense material. I went back and added accessible explanations first, then followed them with the math. For example, before showing the Weibull hazard function formula, I now walk through a concrete scenario comparing two machines with different shape parameters to show why the math matters.

The hardest part was figuring out how much background to include. MaintAlign sits at the intersection of reliability engineering and constraint programming, and both fields have deep literatures. I had to decide what a reader actually needs to understand my project versus what's just interesting context. I ended up keeping four subsections in the State of the Art — classical replacement, imperfect maintenance, Weibull distributions, and constraint programming — because each one maps directly to a design decision in MaintAlign.


#### Content Reflections

I think the motivation section works well now. It starts with the real problem (machines break, production chains amplify the damage) and builds toward why optimization is the right response. The goals are listed as five numbered items, each tied to a specific capability of the system. Professor Kapfhammer's feedback also caught some formatting issues — my LaTeX math wasn't rendering correctly, and I had leftover template phrasing that needed to go. I also added `{#sec-...}` cross-reference labels throughout the chapter so other chapters can point back to specific sections.

The ethical implications section was actually one of the more interesting parts to write. It's easy to think of MaintAlign as just a scheduling tool, but maintenance decisions directly affect worker safety. I tried to be honest about the risks of overconfidence in automated recommendations, especially when input data quality is poor.


### Chapter 2: Related Work

#### Literature Review Process

Finding sources was harder than I expected. My institution requires sources from ACM Digital Library, and ACM DL is genuinely sparse on maintenance optimization — most of that literature lives in operations research and reliability engineering journals. I ended up with a mixed strategy: three papers I found on my own that are core to the maintenance domain (Zhang et al. 2025, Gutschi et al. 2024, Zhao et al. 2019), plus seven ACM-published papers that cover constraint programming for scheduling, simulation-based optimization, and related manufacturing problems. It's not a perfect fit, but it covers the ground MaintAlign needs.

The biggest lesson from the literature review was about organization. My first attempt was basically a paper-by-paper summary — one paragraph per source. Professor Kapfhammer pushed me toward thematic synthesis instead. The final structure groups papers across four themes: scheduling optimization methods, constraint programming as an alternative to metaheuristics, simulation for validation, and bridging maintenance with production planning. That structure made it much easier to show where MaintAlign fits relative to existing work.


#### Content Reflections

The chapter does a decent job of showing the gap MaintAlign fills. No single paper in the review combines CP-SAT-based scheduling, Weibull failure modeling with Kijima imperfect maintenance, opportunistic grouping, and Monte Carlo validation. The closest are Zhang et al. (who integrate maintenance with production but use a metaheuristic) and Lorenzi et al. (who use CP-SAT but for job-shop scheduling, not maintenance). Positioning MaintAlign between those two lines of work felt like the clearest way to justify the project.

One surprising finding was from Gutschi et al. — their result that a predictive maintenance model with poor precision can actually perform worse than no predictive system at all. That reinforced why MaintAlign's Monte Carlo validation layer matters: you can't just trust the optimizer's output without testing it against stochastic failure scenarios.


### Overall Reflections

Chapters 1 and 2 work together in a way I didn't fully appreciate at first. The introduction sets up four technical building blocks (replacement theory, imperfect maintenance, Weibull modeling, constraint programming), and the related work chapter shows how existing papers have used those building blocks in different combinations. The "Positioning MaintAlign" section at the end of Chapter 2 then ties it all together by explaining what combination is new.

The main thing I learned about academic writing is that structure carries a lot of weight. Rearranging the related work from a laundry list into thematic sections didn't change the content much, but it completely changed how readable the chapter was. I also learned to watch out for AI-sounding writing — overly smooth transitions, uniform sentence rhythm, and phrases that sound polished but generic. I try to rewrite connective phrases in my own voice before submitting, even if it makes the prose a little rougher.
